annotations.label: Game theory

anchor.snippet: the study of mathematical models of strategic interactions

annotations.note: Game theory as a discipline is not defined as a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Von Neumann's minimax theorem

anchor.snippet: mixed-strategy equilibria in two-person zero-sum games and its proof by John von Neumann

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Sion's minimax theorem (saddle-point and inf-sup forms), which the file explicitly frames as von Neumann's minimax theorem with von Neumann's matrix-game case as a particular instance.

annotations.label: Zermelo's theorem

anchor.snippet: the optimal chess strategy is strictly determined

annotations.note: No determinacy result for finite two-player games of perfect information exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Waldegrave problem

anchor.snippet: a minimax mixed strategy solution to a two-person version of the card game

annotations.note: No mixed-strategy card-game example is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Borel's minimax theorem (symmetric case)

anchor.snippet: proved a minimax theorem for two-person zero-sum matrix games only when the pay-off matrix is symmetric

annotations.note: The general analytic minimax theorem is proven, but matrix games, mixed strategies, and the symmetric-payoff specialization are not formalized.

annotations.label: Borel's conjecture (disproved)

anchor.snippet: conjectured the non-existence of mixed-strategy equilibria in finite two-person zero-sum games

annotations.note: This is a false historical conjecture and is not a formalized statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: developed a criterion for mutual consistency of players' strategies known as the Nash equilibrium

annotations.note: Nash equilibrium is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash existence theorem

anchor.snippet: non-cooperative game has what is now known as a Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies

annotations.note: Existence of Nash equilibria is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Subgame perfect equilibrium

anchor.snippet: his solution concept of subgame perfect equilibria

annotations.note: Subgame perfect equilibrium is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash equilibrium (formal)

anchor.snippet: A Nash equilibrium is a set of strategies, one for each player

annotations.note: No formal definition of Nash equilibrium exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cooperative vs non-cooperative game

anchor.snippet: A game is cooperative if the players are able to form binding commitments externally enforced

annotations.note: The cooperative/non-cooperative distinction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric game

anchor.snippet: A symmetric game is a game where each player earns the same payoff when making the same choice

annotations.note: Symmetric games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Asymmetric game

anchor.snippet: The most commonly studied asymmetric games are games where there are not identical strategy sets for both players

annotations.note: Asymmetric games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zero-sum game

anchor.snippet: are games in which choices by players can neither increase nor decrease the available resources

annotations.note: Zero-sum games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Poker as a zero-sum game

anchor.snippet: Poker exemplifies a zero-sum game

annotations.note: No poker model is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-zero-sum game

anchor.snippet: are non-zero-sum games, because the outcome has net results greater or less than zero

annotations.note: Non-zero-sum games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constant-sum to zero-sum transformation

anchor.snippet: It is possible to transform any constant-sum game into a (possibly asymmetric) zero-sum game by adding a dummy player

annotations.note: No constant-sum/zero-sum game transformation is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simultaneous game

anchor.snippet: Simultaneous games are games where both players move simultaneously

annotations.note: Simultaneous games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sequential game

anchor.snippet: Sequential games (a type of dynamic games) are games where players do not make decisions simultaneously

annotations.note: Sequential games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfect information

anchor.snippet: A game with perfect information means that all players, at every move in the game, know the previous history of the game

annotations.note: Perfect information is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Imperfect information game

anchor.snippet: An imperfect information game is played when the players do not know all moves already made by the opponent

annotations.note: Imperfect information games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfect-information games

anchor.snippet: Examples of perfect-information games include tic-tac-toe

annotations.note: Games such as tic-tac-toe are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complete information

anchor.snippet: Complete information requires that every player know the strategies and payoffs available to the other players

annotations.note: Complete information is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Incomplete to imperfect information reduction

anchor.snippet: Games of incomplete information can be reduced, however, to games of imperfect information

annotations.note: No reduction between information types is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bayesian game

anchor.snippet: Bayesian game means a strategic game with incomplete information

annotations.note: Bayesian games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bayesian game example

anchor.snippet: where Player 1 is unsure whether Player 2 would rather date her or get away from her

annotations.note: No Bayesian game example is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Combinatorial game

anchor.snippet: Games in which the difficulty of finding an optimal strategy stems from the multiplicity of possible moves are called combinatorial games

annotations.note: Mathlib only has the `GameAdd` subsequency relation modeling combinatorial game addition (for well-founded recursion); it has no definition of combinatorial games themselves.

annotations.label: Continuous game

anchor.snippet: Continuous games allow players to choose a strategy from a continuous strategy set

annotations.note: Continuous games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cournot competition as continuous game

anchor.snippet: Cournot competition is typically modeled with players' strategies being any non-negative quantities

annotations.note: Cournot competition is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Differential game

anchor.snippet: Differential games such as the continuous pursuit and evasion game are continuous games

annotations.note: Differential games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Game with random time horizon

anchor.snippet: A particular case of differential games are the games with a random time horizon

annotations.note: Games with random time horizon are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Evolutionary game theory

anchor.snippet: Evolutionary game theory studies players who adjust their strategies over time according to rules that are not necessarily rational or farsighted

annotations.note: Evolutionary game theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: One-player game

anchor.section: Stochastic outcomes (and relation to other fields)
anchor.snippet: Individual decision problems with stochastic outcomes are sometimes considered

annotations.note: One-player decision problems are not formalized as games in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Partially observable stochastic game

anchor.section: Stochastic outcomes (and relation to other fields)
anchor.snippet: is considered to be partially observable stochastic game (POSG)

annotations.note: Partially observable stochastic games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metagame

anchor.snippet: These are games the play of which is the development of the rules for another game, the target or subject game

annotations.note: Metagames are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mean field game theory

anchor.snippet: Mean field game theory is the study of strategic decision making in very large populations of small interacting agents

annotations.note: Mean field game theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Essential elements of a game

anchor.snippet: To be fully defined, a game must specify the following elements

annotations.note: There is no general definition of a game and its constituent elements in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extensive form

anchor.snippet: The extensive form can be used to formalize games with a time sequencing of moves

annotations.note: Extensive-form games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Backward induction

anchor.snippet: To solve any extensive form game, backward induction must be used

annotations.note: Backward induction for game solving is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extensive-form game example

anchor.snippet: The game pictured consists of two players

annotations.note: No extensive-form game example is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal form

anchor.snippet: The normal (or strategic form) game is usually represented by a matrix which shows the players, strategies, and payoffs

annotations.note: Normal-form (strategic-form) games are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal-form payoff example

anchor.snippet: Suppose that Player 1 plays Up and that Player 2 plays Left

annotations.note: No normal-form payoff example is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extensive-to-normal form equivalence

anchor.snippet: Every extensive-form game has an equivalent normal-form game

annotations.note: No equivalence between extensive and normal forms is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characteristic function

anchor.snippet: In cooperative game theory the characteristic function lists the payoff of each coalition

annotations.note: The cooperative-game characteristic function is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characteristic function (formal)

anchor.snippet: from the set of all possible coalitions of players to a set of payments

annotations.note: No coalition-to-payoff characteristic function is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nash equilibrium (best response)

anchor.snippet: A set of strategies is a Nash equilibrium if each represents a best response to the other strategies

annotations.note: Nash equilibrium and best response are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Evolutionarily stable strategy

anchor.snippet: The best-known equilibrium in biology is known as the evolutionarily stable strategy (ESS)

annotations.note: Evolutionarily stable strategy is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Every ESS is a Nash equilibrium

anchor.snippet: every ESS is a Nash equilibrium

annotations.note: Neither ESS nor Nash equilibrium nor their relationship is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Biological altruism

anchor.snippet: This is a situation in which an organism appears to act in a way that benefits other organisms and is detrimental to itself

annotations.note: Biological altruism is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamilton's rule

anchor.snippet: Hamilton's rule explains the evolutionary rationale behind this selection

annotations.note: Hamilton's rule is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Yao's principle

anchor.snippet: Yao's principle is a game-theoretic technique for proving lower bounds on the computational complexity of randomized algorithms

annotations.note: Yao's principle is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prisoner's dilemma

anchor.snippet: Two members of a criminal gang, A and B, are arrested and imprisoned

annotations.note: The prisoner's dilemma is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dominant strategy in prisoner's dilemma

anchor.snippet: The dominant strategy (and therefore the best response to any possible opponent strategy), is to betray the other

annotations.note: Dominant strategies are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Battle of the sexes equilibria

anchor.snippet: In this game, there are two pure strategy Nash equilibria

annotations.note: The battle of the sexes game and its equilibria are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ultimatum game

anchor.snippet: One player, the proposer, is endowed with a sum of money

annotations.note: The ultimatum game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dictator game

anchor.snippet: Ultimatum game has a variant, that is the dictator game

annotations.note: The dictator game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trust game

anchor.snippet: In the game, one player (the investor) is given a sum of money and must decide how much of it to give to another player (the trustee)

annotations.note: The trust game is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cournot competition model

anchor.snippet: The Cournot competition model involves players choosing quantity of a homogenous product to produce independently and simultaneously

annotations.note: The Cournot competition model is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cournot equilibrium

anchor.snippet: The Cournot equilibrium is reached when each firm operates on their reaction function with no incentive to deviate

annotations.note: Cournot equilibrium is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bertrand competition model

anchor.snippet: The Bertrand competition assumes homogenous products and a constant marginal cost and players choose the prices

annotations.note: The Bertrand competition model is not formalized in Mathlib (Mathlib.NumberTheory.Bertrand is Bertrand's postulate, unrelated).

annotations.label: Bertrand equilibrium

anchor.snippet: The equilibrium of price competition is where the price is equal to marginal costs

annotations.note: Bertrand equilibrium is not formalized in Mathlib.
